title: 1.1: Explains how the old bounce format is processed (e.g., parse_id_from_bounce extracts ID without validation)
1.2: Explains how the new bounce format validates addresses (e.g., HMAC signature verification, returns None for invalid signatures)
1.3: Identifies the OR fallback logic that allows old format to bypass cryptographic validation (e.g., email_log_id = (verp_info and verp_info[1]) or parse_id_from_bounce())
1.4: Reports the SMTP response code for invalid email_log_id (e.g., E512 returns "550 SL E512 No such email log")
1.5: Reports the SMTP response code for valid email_log_id (e.g., E211 returns "250 SL E211 Bounce Forward phase handled" and/or E212 returns "250 SL E212 Bounce Reply phase handled")
1.6: Explains the bounce detection criteria (e.g., is_bounce() checks mail_from == '<>' AND Content-Type == 'multipart/report')
1.7: States that the bounce detection criteria can be spoofed by an attacker (e.g., MAIL FROM:<> and Content-Type header are attacker-controlled)
1.8: Reports what happens when bounce validation requirements are not met (e.g., email is not treated as a bounce, bounce processing is skipped)
1.9: Identifies additional information leakage beyond ID existence (e.g., E211 vs E212 reveals forward vs reply phase, indicating email direction)
